title: Fix the Simple Array Range Finder

statement.markdown: Write a function `findRange` that takes an array of integers as input and returns the longest range of consecutive integers within the array. If the array is empty, return an empty array. The range should be represented as an array of two integers, where the first integer is the start of the range and the second integer is the end of the range.
statement.outputFormat: An array of two integers, e.g., `[3, 15]`
statement.notes: The function should return the longest range of consecutive integers, not necessarily starting from the smallest integer.

examples.explanation: The longest range of consecutive integers is 3 to 15
The longest range of consecutive integers is 1 to 10

hints.text: First hint text: Start by sorting the input array in ascending order

editorial.title: Editorial title
editorial.intuition: Explanation of the correct approach: Sort the array, then use a simple iteration to find the longest range
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach summary: Iterate over the array and check for each element if it's part of a range
editorial.betterSolution: Improve the brute force approach by using a Set data structure
editorial.optimalSolution: Optimal solution details: Sort the array, then use a simple iteration to find the longest range
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Correctness proof: The function returns the longest range of consecutive integers within the array
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run: Find the longest range in the input array [1, 5, 7, 2, 9, 11, 15, 3]
commonMistakes: Mistake 2: Not using a Set data structure
editorial.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting
editorial.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the Set data structure
